name: Wells Canyon
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding in a scenic canyon wash near Spanish Fork with good potential for agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. No permit required and beginner-friendly.
description:
Wells Canyon offers accessible rockhounding on public BLM land in the foothills east of Spanish Fork. The canyon wash is a productive area for collecting colorful agate nodules, banded jasper, and chalcedony pieces that weather out of the hillsides and stream bed. This location is popular with local rockhounders and casual collectors because of its easy access, scenic setting, and no permit requirements. The surrounding terrain features typical Wasatch Range geology with sedimentary and volcanic rock formations that have weathered over time to expose microcrystalline quartz specimens. Expect to spend 2-4 hours walking the wash and nearby hillsides, turning over rocks and searching for the translucent to opaque chalky specimens that indicate agate or jasper.

The collecting is best done by scanning the wash bottom after seasonal water flow and checking loose rocks on both banks. Look for specimens with banding, color variation, or a waxy luster that indicate quality pieces. The canyon is relatively sheltered and experiences less extreme weather than higher elevations, making it accessible most months of the year. Water is not reliably available at the site, so bring your own. The area sees moderate use but remains productive year-round.
history: Wells Canyon sits within the Wasatch Range foothills, formed by Precambrian to Paleozoic sedimentary sequences overlain by Tertiary volcanic deposits. Silica-rich groundwater moving through these layered rocks during diagenesis precipitated agate and chalcedony veins and nodules within the host rock, which have since been exposed by erosion and stream action. This geologic setting is typical of many productive rockhounding areas along the Wasatch Front.
tips: Focus your search in the canyon wash itself, where water erosion naturally concentrates loose specimens. Look for pieces showing white, gray, tan, or banded patterns typical of agate and jasper - avoid specimens that are too soft or crumbly. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and small rock hammer or chisel to gently extract specimens from loose soil without damaging them. Scan the hillside outcrops carefully as they often weather to release fresh material after seasonal rains or freeze-thaw cycles.
terrain_notes: Wells Canyon features a rocky wash with an elevation around 5,400-5,800 feet. The terrain is moderately sloped with scattered sagebrush and juniper vegetation typical of the Wasatch foothills. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting areas is roughly 0.5 to 1.5 miles depending on your chosen route. Footing is uneven with loose rocks and occasional boulders, but no technical scrambling required. The wash bottom is sandy and easier to walk than the adjacent hillsides.
best_season: March - May and September - November. Spring and fall offer comfortable temperatures for hiking and collecting, with lower heat stress than summer months. Winter snow and ice can make access difficult, though mild winter days are sometimes productive.
written_directions: From Spanish Fork, head east on Main Street (UT 51) toward the canyon. Follow UT 51 east approximately 8 miles as it winds into Spanish Fork Canyon. Look for informal pullouts on the north side of the highway near mile marker 8-9, or continue to Forest Service recreation areas with developed parking. Wells Canyon is accessible from side roads off UT 51; local knowledge or detailed offline maps are helpful for pinpointing trailheads. GPS coordinates will be your most reliable navigation aid for reaching this location.
road_conditions: UT 51 is paved and well-maintained. Access roads to parking areas may be unpaved dirt or gravel, typically passable by standard vehicles.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along UT 51 or designated BLM/Forest Service parking areas with variable capacity. No developed lot but adequate space for several vehicles at most trailheads.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Respect the landscape and other users. Pack out all trash.

nearest_services: Spanish Fork, approximately 8 miles west. Grocery stores, restaurants, and gas stations available in town.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain in the wash with uneven rocky footing. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots recommended. The canyon provides some shade from juniper and oak, reducing sun exposure. Beginners and families can enjoy productive collecting along the easier lower wash sections.

faq.question: What types of agates and jasper can I find at Wells Canyon in Utah?
faq.answer: Wells Canyon is known for banded agate, chalcedony nodules, and colored jasper specimens in shades of gray, tan, cream, and sometimes rust. Many pieces show natural banding or color zoning that makes them attractive for lapidary work or display. Specimens range from small nodules (1-2 inches) to larger pieces of 3-4 inches.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Wells Canyon near Spanish Fork?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational collecting on BLM land at Wells Canyon. This is a personal use site with no fees or advance registration needed. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations.

faq.question: How do I find rockhounding spots in Utah County similar to Wells Canyon?
faq.answer: Wells Canyon is one of the more accessible agate and jasper locations near Spanish Fork on public BLM land. The Wasatch Range foothills throughout Utah County have similar geology. Check BLM Utah maps, use GPS coordinates, and ask at local rock shops for recommendations on other productive areas nearby.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Wells Canyon?
faq.answer: March through May and September through November offer the best collecting conditions with comfortable temperatures. Spring collecting benefits from weathering and water action that expose fresh specimens. Avoid summer heat (June-August) and winter snow (December-February) when access may be difficult.

faq.question: Is Wells Canyon beginner-friendly and suitable for families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, Wells Canyon is an excellent beginner location with easy access and low hiking difficulty. The canyon wash is suitable for kids ages 6 and up, and families can find quality specimens without special equipment. Bring water, sun protection, and a small bucket. Stay on established routes and supervise children around loose rocks.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need to rockhound at Wells Canyon?
faq.answer: Bring a small rock hammer or chisel, a 5-gallon bucket, work gloves, sturdy boots, sunscreen, and 2-3 liters of water per person. A magnifying glass helps identify agate luster and banding. Wear long pants for protection against sharp rocks and vegetation. A GPS unit or offline maps are helpful for navigation.
